prompt: An acoustical engineer explains that speech from the adjacent cubicle travels through the air path, while footfall from the floor above is a different mechanism. Which statement correctly distinguishes these sound types?

options.text: Both are impact sounds best measured by IIC
Both are evaluated only with VOC emissions testing
Speech through a partition is airborne sound addressed by STC; footfall through a floor is impact sound addressed by IIC
Speech is impact sound; footfall is airborne sound

explanation: Airborne sound propagates through air and is attenuated by partitions rated with STC. Impact sound is generated by contact with a floor assembly and is rated with IIC. WELL Sound strategies select metrics based on the dominant transmission path.

distractorExplanations.a: Speech through partitions is not impact noise and is not characterized by IIC.
distractorExplanations.b: VOC emissions testing belongs to Materials, not airborne versus impact classification.
distractorExplanations.d: This reverses the standard definitions of airborne and impact sound.

prompt: For a WELL open office targeting acoustic comfort during focused work, which background noise design target is most consistent with common NC guidance for such spaces?

options.text: NC-65 with prominent low-frequency peaks
NC-15 with no ventilation
NC-55 typical of heavy industrial production
NC in the low-to-mid 30s to low 40s range with balanced spectrum, potentially supplemented by masking

explanation: Open offices generally require moderate, balanced background levels—often in the NC 35–45 range depending on program and masking—so HVAC noise does not distract yet can support speech privacy when combined with absorption and layout strategies.

distractorExplanations.a: NC-65 is far too loud for knowledge work environments.
distractorExplanations.b: NC-15 without ventilation is unrealistic and does not reflect operational buildings.
distractorExplanations.c: NC-55 aligns with industrial noise environments, not typical offices.

prompt: A project owner requires third-party verified ingredient disclosure for major furniture purchases. Which documentation path best satisfies WELL Materials transparency expectations?

options.text: Published and third-party verified Health Product Declaration matched to the specific product SKU
Informal vendor email stating 'no harmful chemicals'
STC laboratory report for the chair base
A one-page color brochure without formulation data

explanation: WELL Materials rewards credible, product-specific disclosure. Third-party verified HPDs tied to the exact SKU provide standardized ingredient and hazard reporting that teams can audit against optimization thresholds.

distractorExplanations.b: Unverified statements lack the structured disclosure needed for compliance review.
distractorExplanations.c: STC data does not disclose chemical ingredients or hazards.
distractorExplanations.d: Marketing brochures without formulation detail cannot substitute for recognized disclosure tools.

prompt: A specifier compares two interior adhesives. Product A has very low VOC content in the container. Product B has moderate VOC content but superior CDPH chamber emissions after curing. Which interpretation is most accurate for WELL Materials?

options.text: Only VOC content matters; chamber emissions are irrelevant
Both content and emissions may matter; low content does not guarantee low indoor emissions after application
Emissions testing applies only to exterior paints
The product with higher wet VOC content can never meet Materials goals

explanation: WELL Materials addresses chemical exposure through wet-applied VOC content limits and installed-product emissions testing. Teams evaluate both because solvents in the can may differ from long-term off-gassing behavior after curing.

distractorExplanations.a: Ignoring emissions testing overlooks post-installation occupant exposure pathways.
distractorExplanations.c: Interior installed products and wet-applied goods are central to emissions requirements.
distractorExplanations.d: A product may still meet emissions thresholds if chamber results demonstrate acceptable off-gassing.

prompt: A flooring vendor cites CDPH Standard Method v1.2 compliance. What does this protocol primarily measure?

options.text: Impact Insulation Class of the floor assembly in the field
Thermal transmittance of the slab
VOC emissions from product samples in an environmental chamber over defined time periods
Melanopic equivalent lux at the occupant's eye

explanation: CDPH Standard Method v1.2 is a widely referenced chamber protocol for measuring VOC emissions from building products. Results help teams compare materials for low-emissions selection under WELL Materials.

distractorExplanations.a: IIC is measured with standardized impact sound laboratories, not CDPH chambers.
distractorExplanations.b: Thermal transmittance is unrelated to VOC emissions testing.
distractorExplanations.d: Melanopic lux is a circadian lighting metric from WELL Light concepts.

prompt: Focus rooms in an open office fail to provide confidential speech privacy. Which intervention most directly reduces intelligibility of conversations outside the room?

options.text: Lowering NRC of corridor ceilings to reflect more sound
Removing all door gaskets to improve airflow
Switching to clear glass partitions with no frames
Upgrading door seals, partition STC, and adding controlled sound masking in adjacent areas

explanation: Speech privacy requires reducing sound transmission through partitions and leaks while managing background levels outside the room. High-STC assemblies, tight door seals, and appropriate masking work together to lower speech intelligibility at listeners.

distractorExplanations.a: Lower absorption increases reflection and can worsen corridor speech propagation.
distractorExplanations.b: Removing gaskets increases airborne leakage paths for speech.
distractorExplanations.c: Untreated clear glass provides poor sound isolation compared to laminated or double-glazed acoustic assemblies.

prompt: Before occupancy, WELL Materials requires organized documentation of purchased interior products. What is the main purpose of this pre-occupancy materials documentation?

options.text: To verify that installed products meet disclosure, restriction, and emissions criteria claimed during procurement
To calculate the building's peak cooling load only
To establish NC ratings for the lobby
To replace all acoustic measurements with vendor marketing sheets

explanation: Pre-occupancy documentation confirms that products installed in the project match screened, disclosed, and tested materials approved during design. This closes the loop between specifications and actual installed inventory.

distractorExplanations.b: Mechanical load calculations are unrelated to materials compliance records.
distractorExplanations.c: NC ratings come from acoustic design and measurement, not procurement binders alone.
distractorExplanations.d: Marketing sheets cannot substitute for recognized disclosure and test reports.

prompt: A contractor notes that laboratory STC ratings for a wall assembly may differ from field measurements. Which factor most commonly explains lower field STC performance?

options.text: Field installations often include flanking paths, seal gaps, and workmanship variations not present in ideal lab specimens
STC is only measured outdoors
Laboratory tests always use unfinished concrete floors with no partitions
Field STC is always higher because rooms are larger

explanation: Field Sound Transmission Class accounts for real-world conditions such as incomplete seals, back-to-back outlets, duct penetrations, and structural flanking. WELL teams should detail partitions to minimize paths that undermine laboratory STC ratings.

distractorExplanations.b: STC testing occurs in standardized laboratories and field methods, not exclusively outdoors.
distractorExplanations.c: Laboratory STC tests use defined partition specimens, not open concrete floors without assemblies.
distractorExplanations.d: Room volume alone does not automatically increase field STC; detailing flaws usually lower performance.

prompt: An environmental consultant confirms friable asbestos-containing pipe insulation in a mechanical room scheduled for upgrade. What is the correct project response?

options.text: Allow demolition crews to remove it with standard power tools and no containment
Leave it in place and paint over it while operating fans toward occupied areas
Use licensed abatement contractors, isolation barriers, negative pressure, and proper disposal per regulations
Increase IIC of the floor slab to capture fibers

explanation: Confirmed friable asbestos requires regulated abatement with trained personnel, containment, air monitoring as required, and lawful disposal. WELL projects must protect workers and future occupants from fiber release during renovation.

distractorExplanations.a: Uncontrolled power tool removal illegally disperses fibers.
distractorExplanations.b: Painting without abatement does not prevent release during subsequent disturbance.
distractorExplanations.d: IIC measures impact sound attenuation and has no effect on asbestos fiber control.

prompt: A maintenance crew repairs a door frame coated with pre-1978 paint, generating dust. Which lead-safe practice is most appropriate?

options.text: Use HEPA vacuuming, containment, wet methods, and worker training consistent with lead-safe renovation rules
Dry sand without PPE to finish quickly
Apply sound masking in the corridor to reduce dust
Dispose of dust in open office trash bins

explanation: Disturbing old painted surfaces can create lead dust harmful to workers and occupants. Lead-safe work practices include containment, wet sanding or shaving, HEPA cleanup, and proper waste handling aligned with regulatory requirements.

distractorExplanations.b: Dry sanding without protection increases inhalation and dispersion risk.
distractorExplanations.c: Sound masking does not control particulate lead exposure.
distractorExplanations.d: Open office disposal spreads contaminated dust through the building.

prompt: A classroom retrofit replaces a hard plaster ceiling with acoustic mineral fiber tiles rated NRC 0.85. What improvement should occupants notice most?

options.text: Reduced reverberation and clearer speech conditions due to increased sound absorption
Higher STC between classrooms and the exterior parking lot only
Elimination of all VOC emissions from furniture
Guaranteed IIC 60 for the floor above

explanation: High-NRC ceiling tiles absorb reflected sound energy, shortening reverberation time and improving listening comfort. This is a primary room-acoustic treatment distinct from partition STC or floor IIC.

distractorExplanations.b: Ceiling absorption modestly affects room acoustics but does not primarily set exterior wall STC.
distractorExplanations.c: Ceiling tile changes do not automatically alter furniture VOC emissions.
distractorExplanations.d: Ceiling absorption does not determine floor impact insulation performance.

prompt: A developer asks how carpet affects noise between stacked apartments. Which explanation is most accurate?

options.text: Carpet primarily increases STC of exterior windows
Carpet and underlayment can improve IIC by damping impacts before they reach the structural slab
Carpet eliminates the need for any ceiling treatment below
Carpet replaces CDPH emissions testing for all adhesives

explanation: Soft floor coverings reduce impact energy from footsteps and dropped items, improving IIC metrics. Composite assemblies may still need insulated ceilings or resilient underlayments to meet stringent multifamily goals.

distractorExplanations.a: Carpet on floors does not change exterior glazing STC.
distractorExplanations.c: High IIC goals in multifamily construction often still require ceiling or slab treatments below.
distractorExplanations.d: Carpet selection does not remove adhesive emissions documentation requirements.

prompt: Which scope statement best describes WELL Materials features for a typical office interior project?

options.text: Materials features apply only to structural concrete and steel, not furnishings
Materials features address only exterior landscaping soils
Materials features encompass interior product selection, ingredient transparency, chemical restrictions, and emissions for regularly installed goods
Materials features replace all Sound requirements for HVAC noise

explanation: WELL Materials focuses on interior products occupants contact or inhale from—including furniture, finishes, flooring, and cleaning products—through disclosure, optimization, and emissions verification pathways.

distractorExplanations.a: Furnishings and interior finishes are central to Materials scope, not excluded.
distractorExplanations.b: Landscaping soils are outside typical interior Materials feature scope.
distractorExplanations.d: Materials and Sound are separate concepts with distinct requirements.

prompt: A laminate workstation desktop is evaluated for formaldehyde emissions under WELL Materials. Why is formaldehyde commonly restricted?

options.text: It improves IIC when added to floor adhesives
It is a color rendering metric for daylight
It is required to achieve NC-25 in plenums
It is a known irritant and sensitizer emitted from some wood-based composites and resins

explanation: Formaldehyde off-gasses from certain pressed wood products, adhesives, and coatings. WELL Materials limits emissions to reduce occupant exposure to this respiratory irritant and potential sensitizer.

distractorExplanations.a: Formaldehyde is not used to improve IIC performance.
distractorExplanations.b: Color rendering relates to lighting quality, not formaldehyde chemistry.
distractorExplanations.c: NC targets address mechanical noise spectra, not formaldehyde content.

prompt: An acoustical consultant conducts a pre-design noise survey with sound level meters and octave-band analysis. How should the team use these data in a WELL Sound workflow?

options.text: Map measured levels to floor plans, identify exceedances versus targets, and prioritize acoustic treatments by zone
Archive readings without spatial context
Convert readings directly into HPD hazard listings
Use readings only to size water filtration systems

explanation: Noise surveys support evidence-based design by linking measured spectra and levels to specific spaces. Mapped results inform whether to adjust HVAC, add masking, improve isolation, or increase absorption where occupants experience distraction.

distractorExplanations.b: Data without spatial mapping limits targeted intervention.
distractorExplanations.c: Sound measurements do not generate chemical hazard disclosures.
distractorExplanations.d: Water filtration sizing is unrelated to acoustic surveys.

prompt: A procurement policy restricts phthalates in flexible vinyl flooring and certain upholstery coatings. Which WELL Materials principle does this policy support?

options.text: Maximizing STC between open office neighborhoods
Reducing exposure to prioritized plasticizers associated with health concerns in interior products
Increasing NC ratings in conference rooms
Eliminating the need for cleaning protocols

explanation: Phthalates are plasticizers sometimes found in flexible polymers. WELL Materials optimization encourages avoiding chemicals of concern when alternatives exist, especially in products with frequent occupant contact.

distractorExplanations.a: Phthalate restrictions do not directly change partition sound transmission.
distractorExplanations.c: NC ratings are unrelated to plasticizer chemistry in flooring.
distractorExplanations.d: Cleaning protocols remain necessary regardless of phthalate restrictions.

prompt: Which cleaning frequency practice best aligns with WELL maintenance guidance for reducing pathogen transmission without excessive chemical use?

options.text: Clean high-touch surfaces on a defined schedule with appropriate products while using full-building fumigation daily
Never clean shared keyboards to avoid chemical exposure
Perform routine cleaning of high-touch surfaces such as door hardware, elevator buttons, and shared equipment using approved protocols
Rely only on ambient music to sanitize surfaces

explanation: WELL cleaning protocols balance hygiene and chemical reduction by targeting high-touch surfaces with approved products and schedules. This limits infection risk while avoiding unnecessary building-wide applications of harsh chemicals.

distractorExplanations.a: Daily fumigation introduces excessive chemical exposure beyond targeted needs.
distractorExplanations.b: Skipping shared equipment cleaning increases infection risk.
distractorExplanations.d: Music or masking sound does not sanitize surfaces.

prompt: Supply air diffusers in a private office generate a noticeable rushing noise when the VAV box modulates. Which evaluation approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Measure NC or octave-band levels in the office and compare to targets for the space type
Test VOC content of the diffuser metal only
Measure IIC of the carpet tile
Review HPD for the door hinge only

explanation: Airflow-generated noise is assessed with NC or similar spectra-based metrics in the receiving room. Comparing measured levels to space-type targets identifies whether diffuser selection, duct sizing, or VAV control requires revision.

distractorExplanations.b: Metal VOC content does not diagnose airflow noise.
distractorExplanations.c: IIC evaluates footfall transmission, not supply air turbulence noise.
distractorExplanations.d: Door hinge HPDs do not address HVAC terminal noise.

prompt: A WELL project pursues higher tiers of material health optimization beyond basic disclosure. What additional effort is typically required at advanced tiers?

options.text: Screening more products and avoiding a broader list of chemicals of concern with tighter documentation
Removing all interior partitions to achieve NC-15
Eliminating all furniture to avoid emissions
Replacing acoustic ceiling tiles with polished marble

explanation: Advanced Materials optimization expands the scope and rigor of product screening, requiring more SKUs to meet transparency and restriction criteria. Teams document additional categories and avoid more prioritized substances.

distractorExplanations.b: Partition removal is unrelated to chemical optimization tiers.
distractorExplanations.c: Furniture is required for functional interiors; optimization selects healthier products rather than eliminating all items.
distractorExplanations.d: Polished marble increases reflectivity and may introduce different maintenance and emissions considerations without meeting optimization intent.

prompt: In a multifamily WELL project, demising walls between dwelling units target STC 55 or higher. Which detailing priority best supports achieving the rated performance in the field?

options.text: Align outlets back-to-back without insulation and leave top-of-wall gaps open
Use continuous insulated cavity, staggered studs or double assembly, seal penetrations, and extend partitions to the deck
Install single-layer drywall on one side only
Rely on interior paint color to increase STC

explanation: High STC demising walls require mass, isolation, and airtight continuity. Extending to structure, sealing joints, offsetting outlets, and using insulated decoupled framing reduces flanking that otherwise collapses field performance.

distractorExplanations.a: Back-to-back uninsulated outlets and open gaps create major flanking paths.
distractorExplanations.c: Single-sided thin drywall cannot achieve high STC targets.
distractorExplanations.d: Paint finish color has negligible effect on sound transmission loss.

prompt: A vendor markets an antimicrobial coating for all interior surfaces claiming it eliminates cleaning needs. How should a WELL project team evaluate this claim?

options.text: Accept all antimicrobial additives as automatically beneficial without review
Assume antimicrobial treatments improve STC ratings
Review ingredient disclosure and health evidence cautiously because some antimicrobial treatments add chemicals of concern and do not replace cleaning protocols
Use antimicrobial coatings instead of HPD documentation for all furniture

explanation: WELL Materials encourages scrutiny of treated products because certain antimicrobial additives may introduce hazardous substances without proven long-term health benefit. Cleaning protocols and source control remain essential.

distractorExplanations.a: Blanket acceptance conflicts with WELL emphasis on evidence and ingredient transparency.
distractorExplanations.b: Antimicrobial surface treatments do not function as sound insulation.
distractorExplanations.d: Coatings cannot substitute for furniture ingredient disclosure requirements.

prompt: A hospital-adjacent office build-out occurs while the base building remains occupied. Which construction-phase acoustic strategy best protects occupant comfort?

options.text: Schedule the noisiest demolition when adjacent clinics are busiest to save time
Leave all corridor doors open to vent dust
Ignore noise because construction is temporary
Install temporary barriers, communicate quiet hours, and sequence high-noise work during off-peak periods with monitoring as needed

explanation: WELL Sound considerations extend to construction administration. Temporary acoustic barriers, disciplined scheduling, and monitoring reduce distraction and stress for remaining occupants during renovation.

distractorExplanations.a: Maximizing noise during peak occupancy worsens distraction and complaints.
distractorExplanations.b: Open doors increase noise and dust migration to occupied zones.
distractorExplanations.c: Temporary construction noise still affects well-being and may require mitigation.

prompt: The project team receives HPDs that do not match installed furniture SKUs due to a vendor substitution. What is the best corrective action?

options.text: Obtain HPDs or equivalent disclosure for the exact substituted products before accepting them as compliant
Use the original chair HPD for a different model with alternate foam and fabric
Delete all materials documentation to simplify closeout
Rely on NC measurements of the showroom to prove material health

explanation: Ingredient profiles vary by SKU, foam formulation, and textile treatment. WELL Materials compliance requires documentation tied to the actual installed product, not a visually similar substitute.

distractorExplanations.b: Different models may contain distinct chemicals; mismatched HPDs invalidate verification.
distractorExplanations.c: Deleting documentation prevents certification review and occupant transparency.
distractorExplanations.d: Showroom noise levels do not demonstrate product chemical composition.

prompt: A WELL Mind restorative space is programmed as a quiet room for decompression. Which acoustic design approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Maximize hard reflective surfaces and loud background music
Provide low NC background, absorptive finishes, and isolation from high-activity zones
Place the room adjacent to the fitness studio with no door seals
Use only high-STI speech intelligibility testing as the sole metric

explanation: Restorative spaces support mental recovery through calm acoustics: controlled low background noise, absorption to prevent harsh reflections, and separation from loud adjacencies. This aligns with WELL Sound goals for supportive environments.

distractorExplanations.a: Reflective, loud environments undermine restoration and relaxation.
distractorExplanations.c: Unsealed adjacency to fitness areas imports impact and airborne noise.
distractorExplanations.d: Speech intelligibility metrics alone do not define restorative quiet; broader comfort targets matter.

prompt: An acoustical report lists STC, IIC, NRC, and NC for various building elements. Which pairing of metric to application is correct?

options.text: NRC — airborne transmission through demising walls
NC — VOC emissions from carpet adhesive
STC — airborne partition isolation; IIC — floor impact isolation; NRC — absorption; NC — background noise spectrum
IIC — melanopic lighting at the eye

explanation: Each acoustic metric serves a distinct purpose: STC for airborne partition loss, IIC for floor impact noise, NRC for absorption, and NC for acceptable HVAC and background noise spectra. Using the correct metric avoids misapplied solutions.

distractorExplanations.a: NRC measures absorption within a space, not transmission through walls.
distractorExplanations.b: NC is an acoustic noise curve, not a VOC emissions standard.
distractorExplanations.d: IIC is unrelated to circadian lighting metrics.
